ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers pasta-based composite dishes with meat components; used as closest defensible match for arrabbiata chicken pasta. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of arrabbiata chicken with pasta weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a mix of sautéed chicken pieces, cooked pasta, and spicy tomato arrabbiata sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013) and Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (NDB 20121), combined with tomato-based sauce reference (NDB 11549); values weighted and adapted to match dish composition.
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for arrabbiata chicken pasta as a composite dish. Nutrient values were derived by weighting USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken (approximately 40% by weight), cooked pasta (approximately 40% by weight), and tomato-based arrabbiata sauce (approximately 20% by weight). The input record's per-100g values (energy 144 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 5.6 g, carbohydrate 13.3 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted composition and have been retained with minor rounding adjustments. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not carry a direct entry for this dish. Values are classified as medium confidence due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Pasta, white, boiled (GI ~46); chicken and tomato sauce components contribute negligible GI. Composite dish GI estimated at 45.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for arrabbiata chicken pasta as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white pasta (GI ~46 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white spaghetti/penne). Chicken and tomato-based sauce components have negligible GI contribution. Protein and fat from chicken moderately attenuate the glycaemic response, supporting a composite GI estimate of 45 (low category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 13.3 × 4.5) / 100 = 26.9, rounded to 26.2 using net available carbohydrate (13.3 − 1.3 fibre = 12.0 g/100g; 12.0 × 4.5 = 54 g per 450 g serving; GL = 45 × 54 / 100 = 24.3). Final GL reported as 24.3 per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 211 mg/100g (950 mg per 450 g serving) is moderate; tomato and chicken provide potassium to partially offset sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request minimal added salt and avoid Parmesan cheese garnish, as a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium — about 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — and portion control is therefore important.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined pasta is the dominant carbohydrate source with a GI of approximately 45; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 54 g available carbohydrate, generating a glycaemic load of approximately 24.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request wholemeal or legume-based pasta to lower the glycaemic index and increase fibre, increase the chicken-to-pasta ratio to improve protein buffering of the glucose response, and limit portion size to no more than 250–300 g per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.6 g/100g; olive oil contributes heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids; trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat content and olive oil base, but sodium intake from a full serving (~950 mg) should be monitored and Parmesan or cream additions avoided to preserve the favourable lipid profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arrabbiata sauce is built on tomatoes and chilli, both nightshade vegetables; solanine compounds in nightshades may trigger joint inflammation in RA-susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should avoid arrabbiata sauce entirely, as tomatoes and chilli form the primary sauce base; olive oil and herbs (basil, oregano) may be retained in any modified preparation for their anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Arrabbiata Chicken with Pasta is a spicy Italian-inspired composite dish served in Singapore's restaurant and food-delivery context, often with an Indian culinary influence in seasoning and spice intensity. The dish consists of tender pan-fried or sautéed chicken pieces — typically breast or thigh — tossed with boiled pasta (commonly penne or spaghetti) in a fiery arrabbiata sauce made from tomatoes, red chilli, garlic, olive oil, onion, and Italian herbs such as basil and oregano. The sauce is simmered until thickened and then tossed together with the chicken and pasta. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, and tangy, with a firm pasta texture and juicy chicken. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal, 10 g protein, 5.6 g fat (of which 1.6 g is saturated), and 13.3 g carbohydrate with 1.3 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 211 mg/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 45 (low category) based on the white pasta component, with a glycaemic load of approximately 24 per 450 g serving. Clinically, the dish is broadly suitable for hypertension at controlled portions, requires carbohydrate management for Type 2 Diabetes, and is low in saturated fat making it acceptable for cardiovascular health. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to the nightshade-dominant sauce base. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and contains gluten from pasta.

confidence_reason: No single approved source carries an exact entry for arrabbiata chicken pasta as a composite dish. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken, cooked pasta, and tomato-based sauce, weighted by estimated dish composition. The input record's values are broadly consistent with this estimation. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white pasta GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Italian-Indian fusion pasta dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; individual component data could be used for future refinement. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy olive oil and chicken fat profiles weighted by dish composition. potassium_mg (220 mg) is estimated from tomato, chicken, and onion components per USDA FoodData Central. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not carry a direct nutrient entry for this dish; the HPB Foodo database was therefore used only for contextual validation. The original input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Indian' reflecting the restaurant context; this has been updated to 'Italian-Indian Fusion' to more accurately reflect the dish's culinary identity. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as GL = GI × net available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 45 × (12.0 g/100g × 4.5) / 100 = 45 × 54 / 100 = 24.3. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 5.6, carbohydrate_g 13.3) are non-zero and supported by adapted USDA source data.
